A male narrator's voice, accompanied by a subtle background score, begins to describe how Yellen was able to 'pause the quantitative easing part of the easy money policy without a tantrum,' by maintaining the Fed's massive balance sheet and keeping short-term interest rates low. The visual transitions to a color shot of Yellen seated at a podium with microphones, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'JANET YELLEN, Chair, Federal Reserve Board,' against a dark blue backdrop with the Federal Reserve seal. Her clear, authoritative voice then states, 'The FOMC reaffirmed its view that the current zero to one quarter percent target range for the Federal Funds rate remains appropriate.' Exterior shots of the stately Federal Reserve Building, showcasing its classical architecture with columns and an eagle sculpture under overcast skies, appear briefly with PBS and FRONTLINE logos. The narrator's voice resumes, explaining that the Fed justified its actions because fears of runaway inflation hadn't materialized and economic growth was low. The video returns to a black-and-white shot of Yellen at the podium, holding a pen, as the narrator concludes that Yellen's policy pullback 'didn't dampen concerns and criticisms about the ill effects of the Fed's policies.' The scene then cuts to a black screen displaying 'CHAPTER FIVE' and 'Who Owns the Stocks.' The background score fades as the final scene shifts to a color shot of two men in a modern, warmly lit setting with string lights and wooden walls, one bald and in a dark blue shirt, the other with graying hair in a dark suit, engaged in conversation. The man with graying hair is heard asking, 'So you're doing a documentary on the Fed and...'

The video opens with a close-up of a bald man in a dark blue shirt, seated in a warmly lit, modern outdoor setting with wooden paneling and string lights. He engages in a brief, conversational exchange about "monetary policy" with an older man, asking "We are trying to?" and later agreeing "No, no, no, I think it's a great idea." The camera then cuts to the older man, later identified by a narrator as Nobel Prize-winning economist Joseph Stiglitz, who has graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit over a light blue shirt. Stiglitz speaks earnestly, gesturing with his hands, discussing the Fed's intention to "stimulate aggregate demand." A brief black-and-white archival shot shows Joseph Stiglitz speaking at a podium with a microphone, with text referencing unemployment and economic transition, while the narrator explains Stiglitz's concern that the Fed's actions were "exacerbating economic inequality." The video returns to the present-day setting, alternating between close-ups of the two men as Stiglitz, with an authoritative voice, elaborates on his concerns, stating that the "trickle-down economics" approach and quantitative easing, which lowers interest rates and drives up stocks, primarily benefits "the people in the top," specifically "not just the top 10%, 1%, 1/10th of 1%," thereby increasing inequality "enormously." Throughout, the bald man listens attentively, occasionally nodding or responding, while soft background music underscores the intimate, conversational tone of this deep discussion on economic policy and its societal impact.

The minute opens with a close-up of Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard wearing a dark blue shirt, seated in a warmly lit indoor setting with string lights and wooden walls, listening intently. The scene then cuts to Joseph Stiglitz, an older man with white hair and a beard, dressed in a dark suit over a light blue shirt, identified by a lower-third as Chief Economist at The Roosevelt Institute. He speaks expressively, discussing how increasing wealth inequality since the late 1970s was being put 'on steroids.' James Jacoby's voice is then heard, asking about the response from folks at the Fed. Joseph Stiglitz responds, continuing to speak with an authoritative voice and expressive gestures, stating that the Fed's mandate is to increase employment and use the tools they have. The scene transitions to an over-the-shoulder view of someone watching a laptop screen, where James Jacoby's voice is heard again, introducing Neel Kashkari, the President of the Minneapolis Fed, as the only current Fed official who agreed to speak. The camera zooms in on Neel Kashkari, a bald man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking animatedly during a video call from a well-lit, modern office with vertical blinds and a lamp in the background, gesturing with a pen. He speaks passionately about the Fed's mission to put Americans back to work, help them get their wages up, especially for the lowest income Americans, and emphasizes that the trade-off is well worth it if they can put Americans back to work so they can put food on the table and take care of themselves. The overall atmosphere is professional and focused, with a clear emphasis on expert analysis and dialogue.

event_blocks.summary: A bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue shirt, seated in a modern, softly lit interior, discusses the widening wealth gap and questions the Fed's role, asserting that for many Americans, their job is their most valuable asset, as boosting wages makes this asset more valuable. A CNN news broadcast shows President Barack Obama at a podium with 'JOBS, JOBS, JOBS' and 'UNEMPLOYMENT AT LOWEST RATE SINCE APRIL 2008' on screen, as a crowd cheers "Middle class economics works!"; a female anchor reports on Obama's speech in Wisconsin, highlighting 223,000 jobs added in June, with footage of a Philadelphia Unemployment Project office and a woman in a red dress typing at a home office desk. The man in the dark blue shirt narrates that by 2015, employment numbers were improving dramatically, but critics felt the Fed's focus on jobs missed the full picture, quoting Neil Kashkari who said "a job is a great asset." The scene shifts to Karen Petrou, an older woman with blonde hair, wearing a red blouse, speaking animatedly in a warmly lit room, asserting that the idea of labor income being universally beneficial is a 'we disassumption,' especially for those working multiple jobs, and that "40% of the United States didn't have $400 in a rainy day fund" and were at risk of "imminent financial peril." Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue shirt, listens intently, holding a pen to his lips in contemplation, describing Karen Petrou as an 'unlikely critic of the central bank.' Jacoby outlines the conventional wisdom that 2015-2020 was a period of recovery with record low unemployment, to which Karen Petrou disagrees, explaining that most Americans were 'economically anxious' and that significant percentages of the middle class were skipping medical care, critiquing how "record unemployment was judged" and arguing that conventional methods fail to account for people unable to work due to insufficient wages, emphasizing, "Employment was fine... wages weren't. And people work to eat." The man then asks for further clarification, inquiring, "What was wrong with the models that the Fed was using in order to judge the success of their programs?" The final shot shows the man in the dark blue shirt from the beginning, now seated across from the woman in red, engaged in conversation at a modern table.

He asks probing, inquisitive questions about unemployment and the Fed's economic models, indicating his role as an interviewer or co-discussant.

Golden Retriever.description: A light-colored dog lying calmly on a dark tiled floor, appearing relaxed and adding a domestic element to the professional setting.

Karen Petrou.description: An older woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a red blouse and small silver earrings, speaking with intensity and expressive facial movements in a modern indoor setting. She articulates concerns about financial insecurity, unemployment measurement, and the importance of wages, demonstrating a clear, articulate, and slightly urgent speaking style.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue shirt, seated in a modern, softly lit interior, who is heard discussing the widening wealth gap and questioning the Fed's role in it. The scene cuts to a bald man in a dark suit and light blue patterned tie, speaking with intensity and expressive hand movements, who asserts that for many Americans, their job is their most valuable asset, as they may not own a house, stocks, or a 401k, and boosting wages makes this asset more valuable. The video then transitions to a CNN news broadcast, showing President Barack Obama at a podium with 'JOBS, JOBS, JOBS' and 'UNEMPLOYMENT AT LOWEST RATE SINCE APRIL 2008' on screen, as a crowd is heard cheering "Middle class economics works!" A female news anchor reports on Obama's speech in Wisconsin, highlighting the 223,000 jobs added in June. The broadcast includes footage of a Philadelphia Unemployment Project office with people working at computers, and a woman in a red dress typing at a home office desk. A male voice, likely the man in the dark blue shirt, then narrates that by 2015, employment numbers were improving dramatically, but critics felt the Fed's focus on jobs missed the full picture, quoting Neil Kashkari who said "a job is a great asset." The scene shifts to a woman with blonde hair, wearing a red blouse, speaking animatedly in a warmly lit room, before the final shot shows the man in the dark blue shirt from the beginning, now seated across from the woman in red, engaged in conversation at a modern table.

The segment opens with a close-up of Karen Petrou, an older woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a red blouse and small silver earrings, speaking with intensity and expressive facial movements. She asserts that the idea of labor income being universally beneficial is a 'we disassumption,' especially for those working multiple jobs. The setting is a modern, well-lit indoor space with large windows and wooden paneling. The camera briefly cuts to a golden retriever lying calmly on a dark tiled floor, then to Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue shirt and a watch, seated at a glass table, holding a pen to his lips in contemplation, listening intently. James Jacoby's calm voice describes Karen Petrou as an 'unlikely critic of the central bank,' noting her career advising banks and big investors, with some brief, indistinct background chatter also audible. The camera returns to Karen Petrou, who continues speaking with emphasis, her expression serious, as a lower-third graphic identifies her as 'KAREN PETROU, Author, Engine of Inequality' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. James Jacoby then outlines the conventional wisdom that 2015-2020 was a period of recovery with record low unemployment, asking Karen if she disagreed. Karen Petrou, maintaining her intense demeanor, confirms her disagreement, explaining that most Americans were 'economically anxious' and that significant percentages of the middle class were skipping medical care. Subtle background details like a wooden chair, a glass table with papers, and a glimpse of greenery outside reinforce the calm yet focused atmosphere of a professional interview, with the camera occasionally shifting between the two speakers, capturing their thoughtful interaction.

The video segment opens with a close-up of Karen Petrou, an older woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a red blouse and small silver earrings, speaking with intensity and expressive facial movements in a modern indoor setting. She passionately articulates the widespread financial insecurity in the US, stating that “40% of the United States didn't have $400 in a rainy day fund” and were at risk of “imminent financial peril.” The camera alternates between her and a bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue shirt, who listens intently, occasionally gesturing with his hands and resting his chin in contemplation. He interjects, asking, “What about this idea that there was record unemployment?” Karen responds by critiquing how “record unemployment was judged,” arguing that conventional methods fail to account for people unable to work due to insufficient wages, emphasizing, “Employment was fine... wages weren't. And people work to eat.” The man then asks for further clarification, inquiring, “What was wrong with the models that the Fed was using in order to judge the success of their programs?” The well-lit, contemporary space with wooden paneling and large windows revealing greenery outside provides a calm yet engaged atmosphere for this serious discussion on economic and financial matters.

event_blocks.summary: Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard in a dark blue shirt, listened intently as Karen Petrou, a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair in a red blouse, used an analogy about income disparity involving 'four guys in a bar' and 'Jeff Bezos' to explain how averages can distort statistics, emphasizing the need to assess individual wealth rather than averages to determine national well-being. Following a transition to a black screen with 'CHAPTER SIX' and 'A Missed Opportunity' accompanied by somber music, archival C-SPAN footage showed Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, waving to a crowd and being embraced by a woman in a white dress on stage, while a voiceover noted his populist campaign's focus on the system not working for the poor and middle class, a sentiment Karen Petrou echoed. The segment then showed Trump, at a podium with 'TRUMP' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!' branding, emphatically declaring 'The American Dream is dead.' Christopher Leonard, identified as 'Author, The Lords of Easy Money', explained that societal disparity where 'the middle struggling and the rich realizing almost unimaginable gains' corrodes the civic foundation. Trump, in a dark suit and blue tie, was then seen walking briskly down a hallway, telling reporters, 'We have to clean up the country. Our country is a mess.' Leonard observed that people felt 'the system is rigged,' a sentiment an unidentified male voice confirmed, stating Trump was 'just speaking what we're all thinking.' Leonard further noted the willingness of a large portion of Americans to vote for Trump, whose campaign 'seemed to be burning down the system.' A large rally featured Trump loudly declaring, 'We are going to drain the swamp!' amidst cheers and signs. Leonard commented, 'That doesn't just happen in a vacuum,' before Trump took the stage with Mike Pence, and Trump, gesturing emphatically, declared, 'We are going to fix our inner cities and rebuild...' with American flags and a 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!' banner behind them.

Correspondent James Jacoby.description: A bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue shirt and a watch on his left wrist, seated at a glass table, holding a pen to his lips in contemplation, listening intently to Karen Petrou's economic explanation during an interview in a modern interior with large windows.

Woman in White Dress.description: A woman with blonde hair styled in an updo, wearing a white dress, who embraces Donald Trump on stage during a political event, as seen in C-SPAN archival footage.

Donald Trump.description: A man with light-colored hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking emphatically at a podium with 'TRUMP' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!' branding, flanked by American flags. He is later seen in a blue tie while walking through a hallway and interacting with supporters at a rally, where he speaks loudly and rallies the crowd. His voice is consistently deep, emphatic, and strong.

Karen Petrou.description: An older woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a red blouse and small silver earrings, speaking with intensity and expressive facial movements in a modern indoor setting with large windows revealing greenery outside. She speaks clearly and articulately, using an economic analogy about income disparity to explain her point, and later interjects 'Sadly,' after a male voiceover discusses Donald Trump's populist campaign.

Mike Pence.description: A man with white hair, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, standing silently beside Donald Trump at a podium during a rally, with American flags in the background.

Christopher Leonard.description: A man with light brown hair, wearing a dark suit and tie, speaking in a well-lit indoor setting with soft background lighting and framed artwork, identified by a lower-third as 'Author, The Lords of Easy Money'. His voice is calm and analytical.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard in a dark blue shirt, listening intently with his chin on his hand in a modern, well-lit interior. He is listening to Karen Petrou, a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair in a red blouse, who speaks with intense, expressive facial movements. Karen articulates an economic point, using an analogy about income disparity, stating that if 'four guys in a bar' making $60,000 a year are joined by 'Jeff Bezos' making 'two gazillion dollars,' it doesn't mean the first four are doing better; rather, it 'distorts statistics.' She emphasizes the need to look at 'how much each person has, not at what the averages are,' concluding that if 'four out of five guys in the bar are not doing well, the country isn't doing well.' The scene then transitions to a black screen displaying 'CHAPTER SIX' and 'A Missed Opportunity,' accompanied by the start of somber, atmospheric music. Archival C-SPAN footage then appears, showing Donald Trump in a dark suit and red tie, waving to a crowd as he walks onto a stage flanked by American flags, where he is embraced by a woman in a white dress. A male voiceover explains that 'the growing sense that the system was not working for the poor and middle class became a central theme of Donald Trump's populist campaign,' before Karen Petrou's voice returns, adding 'Sadly,' as the music continues.

The minute opens with Donald Trump, a man with light-colored hair in a dark suit and red tie, speaking emphatically at a podium emblazoned with 'TRUMP' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!', flanked by American flags, declaring in his deep, emphatic voice that "The American Dream is dead." The scene then cuts to Christopher Leonard, identified as 'Author, The Lords of Easy Money', speaking calmly and analytically in a well-lit indoor setting, explaining that "When you have a society with the middle struggling and the rich realizing like almost unimaginable gains, it starts to corrode the civic foundation." The video transitions back to Trump, now in a dark suit and blue tie, walking briskly down a hallway surrounded by aides and security, followed by reporters and cameras. In a close-up, he speaks intensely into a microphone, stating, "We have to clean up the country. Our country is a mess." Leonard's voice returns, discussing how "People start to feel like this cliché you hear all the time that the system is rigged," with an unidentified male voice interjecting, "Like he says, I think the system is rigged," and adding, "He's just speaking what we're all thinking." Leonard continues, noting "the fact that a huge portion of Americans were willing to vote for a president like Donald Trump who's like entire campaign seemed to be burning down the system." The scene shifts to a large, energetic rally where Trump, speaking loudly and rallying the crowd, declares, "We are going to drain the swamp!" amidst loud cheering. He waves to the crowd, many holding 'TRUMP' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!' signs, and interacts with supporters. Leonard's voice briefly returns, stating, "That doesn't just happen in a vacuum," followed by more crowd cheers. Trump then takes the stage with Mike Pence, a man with white hair in a dark suit and blue tie, standing silently beside him. The final shot shows Trump at the podium with Pence, both in dark suits, as Trump gestures emphatically while speaking, his strong voice declaring, "We are going to fix our inner cities and rebuild..." with American flags and a large 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!' banner behind them.

event_blocks.summary: Donald Trump's voice, heard in black-and-white close-ups, passionately listed infrastructure needs like highways, bridges, tunnels, airports, schools, and hospitals, with a narrator explaining this was a moment of potential for the Fed's easy money policies, noting Trump's promise to leverage low interest rates for job creation through infrastructure investment. A CNN news broadcast with a 'BREAKING NEWS' graphic showed Trump at a podium in Trump Tower, New York, signing an executive order on infrastructure, flanked by American flags and the presidential seal, declaring, "We will create millions of new jobs and make millions of American dreams come true." Scenic shots of Washington D.C. at dusk, featuring the Washington Monument reflected in the Tidal Basin and the U.S. Capitol dome silhouetted against a setting sun, were accompanied by the narrator's observation that "once in office, the political paralysis in Washington only intensified." Rana Foroohar, Associate Editor at the Financial Times, explained in a softly lit interview that "Congress simply hasn't been willing to find the amount of money necessary to do it," leading the narrator to conclude that this made "big economic investments all but impossible." Foroohar further detailed the lack of "political cohesion to push through these major programs," and how economists pleaded for "fiscal policy," or "government action to direct this money to the right places," emphasizing, "We can't do all this alone. We can keep rates low, we're trying to keep rates low here."

U.S. Capitol Building.description: An aerial view showing the iconic white dome silhouetted against a vibrant orange sunset, with the sun setting behind it, symbolizing national governance and transition.

CNN News Broadcast.description: A graphic overlay showing the headline 'BREAKING NEWS: TRUMP SIGNS EXECUTIVE ORDER ON INFRASTRUCTURE,' with a timestamp of 4:04 PM ET, and the CNN logo in the corner, indicating a live news report framing Trump's signing event.

Donald Trump.description: A man with light-colored hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, shown in black-and-white close-ups speaking passionately with expressive hand gestures and intense facial expressions, then in color at a podium in Trump Tower, New York, signing an executive order, with American flags and the presidential seal behind him. His distinct voice is heard speaking about infrastructure needs and promising job creation and the fulfillment of American dreams.

Rana Foroohar.description: Associate editor, Financial Times, wearing a light blue turtleneck, speaking in a softly lit interview setting with a colorful abstract poster in the background, her expression engaged and thoughtful, with the FRONTLINE logo appearing in the corner. Her clear, articulate voice discusses the lack of political cohesion for major programs and economists' calls for fiscal policy and government action.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a series of black-and-white close-ups of Donald Trump, his distinct voice passionately listing infrastructure needs such as "highways, bridges, tunnels, airports, schools, hospitals." A male narrator then explains this was "a moment of potential for the Fed's easy money policies," noting Trump's promise to "take advantage of the low interest rates and create jobs by investing in new infrastructure." The scene transitions to a color CNN news broadcast, featuring a 'BREAKING NEWS' graphic, showing Trump at a podium in Trump Tower, New York, signing an executive order on infrastructure, flanked by American flags and the presidential seal. His voice returns, declaring, "We will create millions of new jobs and make millions of American dreams come true." As scenic shots of Washington D.C. at dusk appear, featuring the Washington Monument reflected in the Tidal Basin and the U.S. Capitol dome silhouetted against a setting sun, the narrator's voice observes that "once in office, the political paralysis in Washington only intensified." The video then cuts to Rana Foroohar, Associate Editor at the Financial Times, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit interview setting. Her clear, articulate voice explains that "Congress simply hasn't been willing to find the amount of money necessary to do it," leading the narrator to conclude that this made "big economic investments all but impossible." Foroohar continues, detailing how "there just wasn't the political cohesion to push through these major programs," and how economists were pleading for "fiscal policy," or "government action to direct this money to the right places," emphasizing, "We can't do all this alone. We can keep rates low, we're trying to keep rates low here."

event_blocks.summary: Rana Foroohar, associate editor for the Financial Times, wearing a light blue turtleneck, discussed the difficulty of stimulating spending, noting that "the cheapest money in memory" was not used for infrastructure or education, which would now be "more costly" due to rising interest rates, a point James Jacoby, correspondent, confirmed as a "missed opportunity." Archival footage then showed Donald Trump, Mike Pence, and Paul Ryan outdoors, followed by Trump at a podium, flanked by American flags and officials, proclaiming "the largest tax cut in the history of our country and reform." The Trump administration's tax cut, which "further boosted the markets and deepened economic inequality," saw "economic growth start to tick up during the Trump presidency." In the Oval Office, Trump held up a signed document, as a "BREAKING NEWS" banner indicated the time. To some at the Fed, it seemed "an ideal time to pull back on the easy money experiment," with Jerome Powell being one of them. An exterior shot of the Federal Reserve Building, with its columns and eagle sculpture, preceded a black-and-white shot of Jerome Powell speaking at "The Economic Club of New York," before a title card read "CHAPTER SEVEN: The Fed Blinked." Back at the White House, Trump stood with Powell, announcing, "It is my pleasure and my honor to announce my nomination of Jerome Powell to be the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve. Congratulations, Jay," to which Powell briefly responded, "Thank you, Mr. President." Trump appointed Powell in late 2017. A black-and-white close-up of Powell at a meeting was shown while a narrator described "Jay Powell" as "a profoundly competent, smart guy who has spent his entire career at the nexus of big money and big government," a sentiment echoed by Christopher Leonard, Author of The Lords of Easy Money, speaking in a well-lit indoor setting.

His distinct voice is heard announcing the nomination of Jerome Powell as Chairman of the Federal Reserve.

She speaks clearly and articulately about the challenges of stimulating spending, the missed opportunity to invest when money was cheap, and the increased cost of infrastructure and education reforms now that interest rates are rising.

His voice is heard briefly thanking the President, and he is described by Christopher Leonard as 'a profoundly competent, smart guy who has spent his entire career at the nexus of big money and big government'.

His voice is heard describing Jerome Powell as 'a profoundly competent, smart guy'.

Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard, is seen listening intently, holding a pen to his lips, and then asks in a calm, inquisitive voice if there was a "squandered opportunity here." Rana Foroohar confirms, stating "100%, it was a missed opportunity," and elaborates on how the cheapest money in memory wasn't used for infrastructure and education, which will now be "more costly" due to rising interest rates. The interview takes place in a modern interior with large windows, emphasizing their dialogue. Abruptly, the video shifts to archival footage, accompanied by upbeat, slightly patriotic orchestral music. Donald Trump, Mike Pence, and Paul Ryan are seen walking outdoors. The scene then cuts to Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking at a podium with the presidential seal, flanked by American flags and officials, as an audience applauds. Trump's distinctive voice declares it "the largest tax cut in the history of our country and reform." An unidentified voice then begins to speak about "The marquee legislative..."

The video opens with Donald Trump, in a dark suit and blue tie, speaking at an outdoor podium surrounded by clapping officials and festive red poinsettias. A male narrator's voice discusses the Trump administration's 'achievement' of a 'tax cut that further boosted the markets and deepened economic inequality,' noting that 'economic growth started to tick up during the Trump presidency.' The scene transitions to the Oval Office, where Trump, now in a red tie, holds up a signed document to the camera, with a CNN 'BREAKING NEWS' banner and timestamp. The narrator continues, explaining that 'to some inside the Fed, it seemed like an ideal time to pull back on the easy money experiment,' identifying 'Jerome Powell' as one of them. This is followed by an exterior shot of the grand, neoclassical Federal Reserve Building, featuring columns and an eagle sculpture under an overcast sky, with PBS and FRONTLINE logos visible. A black-and-white shot then shows Jerome Powell speaking at a podium labeled 'The Economic Club of New York,' before a title card appears, reading 'CHAPTER SEVEN: The Fed Blinked.' The scene shifts back to the White House, where Trump stands at a podium with Jerome Powell beside him. Trump's distinct voice is heard announcing, 'It is my pleasure and my honor to announce my nomination of Jerome Powell to be the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve. Congratulations, Jay.' Powell, in a dark suit and blue tie, responds with a brief 'Thank you, Mr. President,' before Trump shakes his hand and steps aside, allowing Powell to address the audience. A narrator confirms that 'Trump appointed Powell in late 2017.' A black-and-white close-up of Powell at a meeting is shown while a new male voice describes 'Jay Powell' as 'a profoundly competent, smart guy who has spent his entire career at the nexus of big money and big government.' The final frames show Christopher Leonard, identified as 'Author, The Lords of Easy Money,' speaking in a well-lit indoor setting, continuing to describe Powell.

event_blocks.summary: Jerome Powell, characterized as a 'self-acknowledged Republican,' 'conservative,' and 'Wall Street guy' with a background in corporate debt and deal-making, stood beside Donald Trump at a White House podium, stating the economy had 'made substantial progress toward full recovery.' Powell was later seen in various formal settings, adjusting his glasses and gesturing while discussing 'extraordinarily accommodative low interest rates' that are 'not appropriate anymore,' as male voices discussed 'The Fed' raising interest rates and reversing quantitative easing, termed 'quantitative tightening' or 'QT.' Exterior shots of the neoclassical Federal Reserve Building, featuring prominent columns and 'FEDERAL RESERVE' engraving, were shown under a partly cloudy sky. A CNN news broadcast from the New York Stock Exchange reported a 'brutal week in the market,' with 'The Dow' closing down 'more than 500 points' and both 'The Dow and the S&P' heading for their 'worst December since the Great Depression,' as indicated by on-screen graphics. Amidst a 'short circuits' sound effect, an off-screen voice remarked on the market's 'tantrum' and warned that 'the decline will accelerate if J. Powell doesn't walk things back.' The segment concluded with a view of the bustling NYSE trading floor, where traders in blue jackets and headsets worked intensely amidst monitors displaying real-time financial data.

It is the location from which a female correspondent reports on the market's downturn, and where the 'global financial system short circuits' is audibly described.

He is described by an off-screen voice as a 'self-acknowledged Republican,' 'conservative,' embracing a 'deregulatory view,' and a 'Wall Street guy' who 'came up through the business of corporate debt and deal-making.'

Stock Exchange Traders.description: Multiple traders in blue jackets and headsets, actively shouting and gesturing on the trading floor, surrounded by electronic displays and monitors, capturing the intensity of financial markets.

Donald Trump.description: Wearing a dark suit and blue tie, standing beside Jerome Powell at the White House podium, listening attentively with a serious expression.

Federal Reserve Building.description: A stately, classical-style building shown from the exterior, featuring prominent columns, engraved 'FEDERAL RESERVE' lettering, and an eagle sculpture atop, under an overcast sky with the American flag flying, representing 'The Fed' which is discussed in the audio regarding interest rate hikes and quantitative tightening.

raw_segments: The video opens with Jerome Powell, described by an off-screen voice as a 'self-acknowledged Republican,' 'conservative,' and 'Wall Street guy' who 'came up through the business of corporate debt and deal-making,' standing beside Donald Trump at a White House podium with the Presidential Seal. Powell, in a dark suit and blue tie, states that the economy 'has made substantial progress toward full recovery.' The scene transitions to close-ups of Powell in various formal settings, adjusting his glasses and gesturing while speaking at events like The Economic Club of New York and The Atlantic Festival, where he discusses 'extraordinarily accommodative low interest rates' that are 'not appropriate anymore.' Throughout, male voices discuss 'The Fed' continuing to raise interest rates, reversing QE, and calling it 'quantitative tightening' or 'QT.' Exterior shots of the neoclassical Federal Reserve Building, with its prominent columns and 'FEDERAL RESERVE' engraving, are shown under a partly cloudy sky. The narrative then shifts to a CNN news broadcast from the New York Stock Exchange, where a female correspondent reports on a 'brutal week in the market,' stating that 'The Dow' closed down 'more than 500 points today' and that 'The Dow and the S&P' are on track for their 'worst December since the Great Depression.' On-screen graphics reinforce this, highlighting 'STOCK MARKET JUST HAD ITS WORST WEEK IN A DECADE' and 'STOCKS HAVE UGLIEST DECEMBER SINCE THE GREAT DEPRESSION,' alongside a graph showing a steep decline in the Dow Jones Industrial Average. An off-screen voice remarks, 'Once again, the market threw a tantrum,' followed by a dramatic 'short circuits' sound effect as another voice declares, 'The global financial system short circuits,' and warns that 'the decline will accelerate if J. Powell doesn't walk things back.' The segment concludes with the bustling trading floor of the NYSE, showing traders in blue jackets and headsets actively working amidst multiple monitors displaying real-time financial data, capturing the intensity of the market's turmoil.

event_blocks.summary: Donald Trump, in a black-and-white shot, gestured emphatically while a commentator noted his "tantrum," before transitioning to color where Trump, at a podium in the Oval Office with a CNBC banner reading 'TRUMP: THE FED HAS 'GONE CRAZY'', declared, "I think the Fed has gone crazy," blaming the Federal Reserve for a market correction and urging them to "lower rates and they would stop quantitative tightening." Outdoors in Morristown, New Jersey, in front of Marine One, a CNN banner stated 'TRUMP ON TRADE WAR: CHINA HAS RIPPED US OFF'. Trump's tweets criticizing the Federal Reserve, with text highlighting his claims that 'The Fed is like a powerful golfer who can’t putt!' and 'They don’t have a clue!', were shown as a commentator explained his frequent attacks on the Fed Chair on Twitter for raising interest rates, suggesting these decisions would harm the economy. A pensive Jerome Powell, adjusting his watch, was shown in black-and-white as a commentator noted, "Powell would change course." On a PBS News Hour set, a female news anchor announced, "The Federal Reserve cut a key short-term interest rate today, after raising it as recently as December," with a graphic of the Federal Reserve Building and 'RATE CUT' behind her. Dion Rabouin from The Wall Street Journal described this as a "complete reversal" and "capitulation to financial markets," explaining that "Financial markets don't like this, so the Fed's going to reverse course," a sentiment that has "defined Chair Powell ever since then." The video concluded with a stern-faced Jerome Powell in black-and-white, as a commentator mentioned the "tricky balancing act for Chairman Powell." The segment then shifted to a black-and-white close-up of Jerome Powell, looking stern, as a male voice discussed criticism that the Fed "bowed to pressure from the White House, or Wall Street, or both," and "sacrificing the central bank's precious independence." A live CNN broadcast showed a male anchor reporting 'U.S. FED CUTS RATES, CITING 'GLOBAL DEVELOPMENTS'', against a city skyline. Jim Chanos, Founder of Kynikos Associates, explained that "The Fed blinked, and the Fed reversed course when the market was down sort of 20% and went from tightening policy to easing policy," concluding that "saving the stock market was now one of the Fed mandates" with "really ominous ramifications for the future," while Correspondent James Jacoby listened intently. An eerie drone sound accompanied a title card reading 'CHAPTER EIGHT — A Giant Bloodsucker'. Exterior shots of the Federal Reserve Building at twilight were shown as a narrator explained that "By 2019, the Fed's easy money experiment had been going on for a decade," with another male voice interjecting that "the Fed's job isn't to help the President of the United States," before the narrator continued, describing how "what it started out as an emergency measure to save the economy had become the status quo." A different male voice added that "quantitative easing is there, but it's a tool you don't want to overdo." The narrator highlighted how this was "deepening the concerns about how the Fed was fueling troubling trends," concluding with "taking advantage of the Fed's low rates" as a Blackstone sign appeared, framed by blurred yellow flowers and passing pedestrians. The video then depicted a formal business meeting in a modern conference room, where professionals reviewed documents against a city skyline, as a male narrator explained how private equity firms were buying up huge swaths of the economy with borrowed money, punctuated by rapid-fire financial figures and the declaration "this is a bargain." The narration described the concentration of wealth and ownership, transitioning to people outdoors holding numbered application forms, followed by hands exchanging documents at a desk, and a high-angle view of a corporate meeting room. An exterior shot of a building with a 'Birds Eye Foods' sign appeared, as a corporate voice detailed their strategy of buying and selling companies like "Birds Eye Foods," "Michael Stores," and "Hilton" to "grow something faster" or "fix whatever that is that's broken." This was followed by a bustling New York City street, professionals watching a presentation, and a man speaking seriously. An aerial view of a complex highway interchange at sunset symbolized connectivity, as the narrator mentioned the Fed's policies fueling a frenzy in Silicon Valley, leading to a modern, open-plan office space. A news anchor in a studio with a 'Delivery Deja Vu' graphic reported on venture capitalists pumping "nearly half a billion dollars into the food delivery startup industry." A news desk with two anchors discussed current events, with the female anchor announcing WeWork's "massive 4.4 billion dollars investment from SoftBank Group" and Airbnb being "now valued at 10 billion dollars." The final scenes returned to the aerial highway view at sunset, ending with a close-up of a hand holding a smartphone displaying a financial chart, as the narration concluded on enabling certain tech companies.

His audio describes the Fed's actions as a 'complete reversal' and 'capitulation to financial markets,' explaining that 'financial markets don't like this, so the Fed's going to reverse course,' and that this has 'defined Chair Powell ever since then.'

raw_segments: The segment opens with a black-and-white shot of Donald Trump gesturing emphatically at a table, as a commentator states, "The President threw a tantrum too." The scene transitions to color, showing Trump at a podium in the Oval Office, with a CNBC banner reading 'TRUMP: THE FED HAS 'GONE CRAZY'', where Trump's voice is heard declaring, "I think the Fed has gone crazy," and attributing a market correction to the Federal Reserve. He continues, "If the Fed knew what it was doing, they would lower rates and they would stop quantitative tightening." Trump then appears outdoors in Morristown, New Jersey, in front of Marine One, with a CNN banner stating 'TRUMP ON TRADE WAR: CHINA HAS RIPPED US OFF'. The video cuts to a series of Trump’s tweets criticizing the Federal Reserve, with text highlighting his claims that 'The Fed is like a powerful golfer who can’t putt!' and 'They don’t have a clue!', while a commentator explains, "The President has been attacking the Fed Chair on Twitter very often for raising interest rates," and suggesting the Fed's decisions would hurt the economy. A black-and-white close-up of Jerome Powell, the Fed Chair, looking pensive and adjusting his watch, is shown as a commentator notes, "Powell would change course." Next, a female news anchor in an orange blouse on a PBS News Hour set, with a graphic of the Federal Reserve Building and 'RATE CUT' behind her, announces, "The Federal Reserve cut a key short-term interest rate today, after raising it as recently as December." Dion Rabouin from The Wall Street Journal, speaking in a warmly lit indoor setting, describes this as a "complete reversal" and "capitulation to financial markets," stating, "Financial markets don't like this, so the Fed's going to reverse course," and that this has "defined Chair Powell ever since then." The video concludes with a black-and-white close-up of Jerome Powell with a stern expression, as a commentator mentions the "tricky balancing act for Chairman Powell."

The minute opens with a black-and-white close-up of Jerome Powell, the Federal Reserve Chair, looking stern and contemplative in glasses and a dark suit, as a male voice immediately begins discussing "criticism that the Fed has bowed to pressure from the White House, or Wall Street, or both," and "sacrificing the central bank's precious independence." This transitions to a live CNN news broadcast where a male anchor, in a blue suit and tie, delivers breaking news: 'U.S. FED CUTS RATES, CITING 'GLOBAL DEVELOPMENTS'', set against a vibrant city skyline at dusk. The scene then shifts to an interview with Jim Chanos, Founder of Kynikos Associates, who, with a serious demeanor, explains in a calm, analytical voice that "The Fed blinked, and the Fed reversed course when the market was down sort of 20% and went from tightening policy to easing policy," concluding that "saving the stock market was now one of the Fed mandates" with "really ominous ramifications for the future." Correspondent James Jacoby, bald with a beard, is seen listening intently, holding a pen to his lips. An eerie, low-frequency drone sound then accompanies a title card reading 'CHAPTER EIGHT — A Giant Bloodsucker'. Exterior shots of the Federal Reserve Building at twilight, with its classical architecture and American flag, are shown as a narrator's voice explains that "By 2019, the Fed's easy money experiment had been going on for a decade." Another male voice interjects that "the Fed's job isn't to help the President of the United States," before the narrator continues, describing how "what it started out as an emergency measure to save the economy had become the status quo." A different male voice adds that "quantitative easing is there, but it's a tool you don't want to overdo." The narrator then highlights how this was "deepening the concerns about how the Fed was fueling troubling trends," concluding with "taking advantage of the Fed's low rates" as the final shot reveals a Blackstone sign, framed by blurred yellow flowers and passing pedestrians.

The video opens with a formal business meeting in a modern conference room, where professionals in suits review documents against a city skyline, as a male narrator explains how private equity firms have been buying up huge swaths of the economy with borrowed money, punctuated by rapid-fire financial figures like "110,000" and "128,000," and the declaration "this is a bargain." The narration continues, describing the concentration of wealth and ownership from houses to hospitals, while the scene transitions to a group of people outdoors holding numbered application forms, suggesting a job or benefit process, followed by hands exchanging documents at a desk. A high-angle view of a corporate meeting room with participants using laptops emphasizes collaboration. An exterior shot of a building with a 'Birds Eye Foods' sign appears, as a corporate voice details how they "come in, buy either real estate or companies" like "Birds Eye Foods," "Michael Stores," and "Hilton," aiming to "grow something faster" or "fix whatever that is that's broken, and then sell it." This is followed by a bustling New York City street, then professionals watching a presentation, and a man in a suit speaking seriously. An aerial view of a complex highway interchange at sunset symbolizes connectivity, as the narrator mentions the Fed's policies fueling a frenzy in Silicon Valley. This leads to a modern, open-plan office space, then a news anchor in a studio with a 'Delivery Deja Vu' graphic, where a male news anchor reports on venture capitalists pumping "nearly half a billion dollars into the food delivery startup industry." A news desk with two anchors, a female news anchor and a male news anchor, discusses current events, with the female anchor announcing WeWork's "massive 4.4 billion dollars investment from SoftBank Group" and Airbnb being "now valued at 10 billion dollars." The final scenes return to the aerial highway view at sunset, before ending with a close-up of a hand holding a smartphone displaying a financial chart, as the narration concludes on enabling certain tech companies.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a distorted image and a male narrator's voice stating the goal is "to disrupt and dominate entire industries without ever turning a profit," transitioning to a CNBC news segment where a female anchor reports on WeWork’s upcoming stock listing, highlighting its "total opportunity is $3 trillion." As nighttime aerial views of New York City, branded with PBS and FRONTLINE logos, are shown, the male narrator explains that the "most destabilizing consequence to the economy" was how the Fed's low interest rates incentivized public companies to accumulate debt. A dimly lit street outside the New York Stock Exchange is shown, followed by a CNBC segment featuring a bald man with glasses discussing elevated valuations and corporate debt. A Fox Business segment with a woman in a striped top and a male voice emphasizes the "rise in corporate debt" and "entirely too much corporate debt out there." A Washington Post headline about corporate debt nearing $10 trillion is displayed, with the narrator detailing "numerous studies and reports" on companies becoming so leveraged that their credit ratings plummeted. A man in a dark shirt is seen writing in a notebook outdoors, as the narrator explains that borrowed money, intended for good use, was instead used differently. The scene shifts to the Federal Reserve Building, then to a Fox Business anchor, and finally a glitchy transition to a man discussing Apple’s $100B stock buyback, accompanied by voices exclaiming, "Buybacks! Buying back stock! Stock buybacks! Stock buybacks robbing the American worker!" A financial news graphic titled 'American Doubles Buyback' shows a stock chart with a year-to-date decline, and a male voice explains companies were borrowing to buy back stock, increasing share value. An exterior shot of a Walmart store with a 'STOCK ALERT' banner is shown, reinforcing the idea that "all that matters is the stock price." A Black man in a light blue dress shirt and patterned tie directly addresses the camera, contrasting past Fed rate cuts that spurred hiring and expansion with current practices where businesses use borrowed money for stock buybacks and technology that eliminates workers. He notes that borrowed money is also used for CEO bonuses, leading to an "endless cycle of things that are designed to increase the stock price rather than improve the..."

raw_segments: The video opens with a glitchy, distorted image of a woman in a black blazer, as a male narrator's voice states the goal is "to disrupt and dominate entire industries without ever turning a profit." This transitions to a clear CNBC news segment where a female anchor with blonde hair, wearing a black dress, reports on WeWork’s upcoming stock listing under the ticker symbol 'WE', explaining its "total opportunity is $3 trillion... 3 and a half percent of the entire world's GDP." Background music begins as the scene shifts to a nighttime aerial view of the New York City skyline, with the Empire State Building illuminated and a full moon overhead, branded with the PBS and FRONTLINE logos. The male narrator continues, describing "the most destabilizing consequence to the economy" as "how the Fed's low interest rates had been incentivizing public companies to take on more and more debt." The camera moves to a dimly lit, cobblestone street outside the New York Stock Exchange, with pedestrians passing the neoclassical building. The video cuts to a previously recorded CNBC segment where a bald man with glasses in a dark suit discusses how "valuations are generally elevated, especially corporate debt." This is followed by a Fox Business segment featuring a woman in a striped top, who, along with another male voice, highlights the problem, stating, "We have flagged the rise in corporate debt" and "We have entirely too much corporate debt out there." A close-up of a Washington Post headline about corporate debt nearing $10 trillion is shown, as the male narrator details "numerous studies and reports detailing the extent of the debt" and how "even marquee companies were becoming so leveraged their credit ratings plummeted." A man in a dark shirt is then seen seated at a table outdoors, pensively writing in a notebook, while the narrator explains, "The Fed had hoped that companies would put all that borrowed money to good use... But in reality, it played out differently." The scene transitions to the Federal Reserve Building at dusk, with cars passing by, then a close-up of its facade with the eagle emblem and 'FEDERAL RESERVE' engraved. The video ends with a Fox Business anchor in a suit, followed by a glitchy transition to a man in a suit with a banner about Apple’s $100B stock buyback, accompanied by a rapid-fire montage of voices exclaiming, "Buybacks! Buying back stock! Stock buybacks! Stock buybacks robbing the American worker!"

The segment opens with a financial news broadcast graphic titled 'American Doubles Buyback,' displaying a stock chart showing an 11.02 point (20.55%) year-to-date decline from March to July. A ticker of currency pairs and top news headlines, including 'Fed Cuts Rates' and 'Walmart Jumps 4%,' are also visible. A male voice immediately begins explaining how "companies were often borrowing money to buy back their own stock, making the remaining shares more valuable and the prices higher." The scene then transitions to an exterior shot of a Walmart store, with a red 'STOCK ALERT' banner announcing 'WALMART JUMPS ▲ 4%' and real-time market indices. The male voice continues, stating, "As a corporation, you realize all that matters is the stock price. So, what do we have to do to increase the stock price? And more often that is buying back the stock." The video cuts to a Black man, wearing rectangular glasses, a light blue dress shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting with a blurred background. He gestures occasionally with animated expressions as he elaborates on the shift in corporate behavior: "it used to be the Fed would lower interest rates, businesses would then take on more debt... to hire more workers, build more machines and more factories." He then contrasts this with current practices: "Now what happens is the Federal Reserve lowers interest rates, businesses use that to go out and borrow more money, but they use that money to buy back stock and invest in technology that will eliminate workers and reduce employee head counts." The camera alternates between front-facing and side-profile shots of him. A brief cut shows a bald man in a dark shirt, looking down with a serious, contemplative expression, before returning to the speaker. The speaker continues, explaining how companies also use borrowed money "to give the CEO and other corporate officers big bonuses, and then eventually, issue more debt and buy back more stock." He concludes by describing this as "this endless cycle of things that are designed to increase the stock price rather than improve the..."

event_blocks.summary: The segment begins with a montage of financial news broadcasts from Bloomberg and Fox Business, displaying stock market data and headlines about buybacks, with voices discussing corporate buybacks and their impact on stock prices. Sheila Bair, former Chair of the FDIC, in a softly lit interview room, criticizes buybacks as an embarrassment, attributing them to the Fed's incentivizing of bad behavior on Wall Street due to low interest rates, which she sees as a drag on economic growth. A Fox Business broadcast features anchors reporting on market data, including GE, and discussing Warren Buffett's approval of Apple's buybacks. Rana Foroohar, from the Financial Times, in a softly lit room, argues that easy money leads to a financialized economy that primarily benefits itself by buying and selling existing assets. Dynamic cityscapes of a subway station, Times Square, and Wall Street, including the New York Stock Exchange, are shown with subtle, dramatic music. Foroohar describes the easy money and financialized economy as a "Faustian bargain." The scene shifts to Wall Street, followed by a woman in a light blue turtleneck explaining how pension funds and 401Ks contribute to the cycle. The Charging Bull statue is shown at night. Jeremy Grantham, a legendary investor, in a warmly lit room, describes the finance industry as a "giant bloodsucker" that has more than doubled in size, extracting wealth from the rest of the economy without generating real income. Correspondent James Jacoby, in a modern interior, questions Neel Kashkari, President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, about corporate buybacks and debt. Kashkari, in a bright, modern room, expresses concern that companies are buying back stock because they lack profitable investment opportunities. The segment concludes with Jacoby participating in a video call with Kashkari, who asserts that the Fed's policies unintentionally help the rich by creating the perception that Wall Street is insulated from risk, despite the Fed's well-intentioned policies, and that Congress and regulators should address concerning trends.

Male anchors are heard discussing Warren Buffett's approval of Apple's stock buybacks, noting a $100 billion buyback.

She speaks thoughtfully about how low interest rates and corporate buybacks fail to create real wealth, jobs, or improve the labor market, instead acting as a 'drag on our economic growth'.

Rana Foroohar.description: Associate editor, Financial Times, wearing a light blue turtleneck, speaking in a softly lit interview setting with a colorful abstract poster in the background, her expression engaged and thoughtful, with the FRONTLINE logo appearing in the corner. She articulates how an 'age of easy money' fosters a 'financialized economy' focused on buying and selling assets rather than supporting real businesses and people, describing it as a 'diabolical' 'Faustian bargain' that we are all part of, where the real economy is not truly benefiting.

Identified as legendary investor Jeremy Grantham, he speaks with a distinct British accent, critically describing the finance industry as 'a giant bloodsucker' that has 'more than doubled in size' and is 'sucking more than twice the blood out of the economy,' emphasizing that 'we do not generate any widgets... We are just a cost.'

raw_segments: The minute opens with a rapid montage of financial news broadcasts from Bloomberg and Fox Business, showcasing dynamic stock market data including indices like the DOW, S&P 500, and NASDAQ, alongside real-time updates on companies such as General Electric (GE) and Adobe Systems (ADBE). On-screen tickers, stock alerts, and headlines like 'BUYBACK BOOM' and 'DOW FINISHES AT HIGHEST LEVEL SINCE MARCH 12' flash across the screen. Male voices from the broadcasts discuss financial activity, with one stating, "GE just authorized a 50 billion dollar stock buyback," and another adding that "the numbers were astounding, more than 6 trillion dollars in corporate buybacks during this easy money decade after the financial crisis," emphasizing the "big difference to the stock price." The scene then transitions to a more intimate setting: a softly lit interview room where professional camera equipment surrounds Sheila Bair, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair wearing a textured gray sweater. Identified by a lower-third graphic as 'SHEILA BAIR, Chair, FDIC, 2006-11' for 'FRONTLINE', she speaks with expressive gestures and a serious, articulate demeanor. Her voice states, "Buybacks were an embarrassment," describing them as an example of things "just going mainstream." A male narrator then introduces her, noting she "was issuing public warnings at the time that the Fed was incentivizing bad behavior on Wall Street despite its best intentions." Sheila Bair continues, explaining, "I can't fault the companies so much because these interest rate, this interest rate environment creates very strong economic incentives to do exactly what they're doing." She elaborates on the difficulty of innovation, saying, "It's hard to create a new product, it's hard to come up with a new idea for a service, it's hard to build a plant and hire people and run your organization. It's real easy to issue some debt."

The video opens with a close-up of a bald man in a dark blue shirt, intently focused on a silver MacBook Air. The scene transitions to Sheila Bair, identified as Chair of the FDIC from 2006–11, with shoulder-length brown hair and a gray sweater, speaking thoughtfully in a softly lit interview setting. She explains how companies paying out shareholders and boosting share prices "doesn't create real wealth, it doesn't create real opportunity, it doesn't create jobs, it doesn't improve the labor market," calling low interest rates a "drag on our economic growth, not a benefit." The narrative then shifts to a Fox Business broadcast, where a female anchor in a black blazer and pink top reports live from a studio with a 'DOW POSITIVE FOR 2018' banner, while a male anchor in a blue suit and glasses gestures toward a screen displaying 'S&P WINNERS' and stock data, including GE. A male voice is heard discussing Warren Buffett's approval of Apple's buybacks, with another male voice adding that a shareholder would naturally support buying back "$100 billion in stock." The broadcast cuts to Rana Foroohar, Associate Editor at the Financial Times, wearing a light blue turtleneck, speaking in a softly lit room with a colorful abstract poster behind her. She states that an "age of easy money" leads to a "financialized economy that's really more in service to itself," primarily "buying and selling existing assets rather than helping real businesses and real people make real investments." The video then transitions to dynamic cityscapes: a bustling subway station with moving escalators, crowded Times Square with bright billboards, and a wide-angle shot of Wall Street with the New York Stock Exchange building and American flags, all accompanied by a subtle, dramatic background music track. The sequence ends with a return to Rana Foroohar, who continues her commentary, describing the "diabolical" nature of easy money and the financialized economy as a "Faustian bargain" where "we're all in it," pretending "something wonderful happening in the real economy when really it's just..." before her audio fades.

The minute opens with a wide, sunlit shot of Wall Street, showcasing the grand neoclassical facade of the New York Stock Exchange, bustling with pedestrians and flanked by towering skyscrapers, establishing the financial heart of New York City. A woman in a light blue turtleneck then speaks calmly in a softly lit indoor setting, explaining how 'everybody's money is kind of helping to push this whole cycle along' through pension funds and 401Ks. The scene transitions to the Charging Bull statue at night, illuminated under streetlights, symbolizing market strength, with an 'Evacuation Day' street sign visible in the background. Hands are then seen typing on a MacBook Air in a home or office workspace, with a white mug blurred in the background. A male voice (narrator) states that 'even some of the largest beneficiaries of this trend told me it made them uncomfortable,' introducing 'legendary investor Jeremy Grantham.' The camera then focuses on an older man with white hair, wearing a dark suit, striped shirt, and blue patterned tie, speaking with a serious yet composed demeanor in a warmly lit room. Identified as Jeremy Grantham, he speaks with a distinct British accent, detailing how the percentage of GDP going to finance has risen from 'three and a half to eight and a half.' He vividly describes the finance industry as 'a giant bloodsucker' that has 'more than doubled in size' and is 'sucking more than twice the blood out of the rest of the economy,' asserting, 'we do not generate any widgets, we do not generate any real increase in income. We are just a cost.' As he concludes, a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, is seen looking down thoughtfully in a different indoor setting, possibly listening, as a male voice (likely the narrator/interviewer) asks for clarification: 'When you say we, you mean you and other members of the financial community have been this kind of bloodsucker on the...' before the audio cuts off.

The video opens with a close-up of Jeremy Grantham, an older man with white hair, wearing a dark suit, a blue and white striped shirt, and a blue patterned tie, speaking with a serious and composed demeanor in a warmly lit room. He articulately explains how the financial industry, despite fulfilling a necessary service, has created "layers upon layers of more and more convoluted, expensive financial instruments" to generate profits, and through "ingenuity and salesmanship" and "lobbying in Congress," has imposed the idea that banking and finance are "utterly important at all times," warning that if challenged, "the entire economy will collapse in ragged disarray." The FRONTLINE logo appears, identifying him as Co-founder of GMO LLC. The camera then cuts to Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, seated in a modern interior with large windows, holding a pen to his lips in contemplation, as he inquisitively asks about "corporate buybacks, the elevation of corporate debt," and how it's viewed by the Fed. The scene briefly returns to Grantham, who listens with subtle head movements and expressive facial gestures, before cutting to a laptop screen displaying Neel Kashkari, a bald man in a dark suit and light blue patterned tie, speaking animatedly with hand gestures. Kashkari, identified by a lower-third as President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, explains with emphasis that when companies buy back stock, it indicates they "don't have profitable places to invest," finding it "easier for us just to buy back our stock," which he deems "concerning." The camera shifts to Jacoby, listening intently, then cuts back to Kashkari, who continues speaking with expressive hand movements in a bright, modern room with vertical blinds and a lamp in the background, emphasizing the concerning nature of this trend. The sequence suggests a structured interview or discussion between financial experts, with Jacoby acting as the interviewer, and the setting transitions between a warm, intimate room for Grantham and a bright, professional space for Kashkari, emphasizing the contrast in tone and environment.

The segment opens with a close-up of a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, seated in a softly lit, modern room with natural light and a plant. He is intently focused on a laptop screen, participating in a video call with Neel Kashkari, President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis. Kashkari, bald and in a dark suit with a light blue patterned tie, speaks animatedly from a bright, modern room, stating that the future of the economy isn't solely controlled by the Fed. A narrator then explains that Kashkari believes Congress and regulators should address concerning trends, and that he has previously disputed criticisms that the Fed's policies primarily boost financial markets and help Wall Street. The camera alternates between close-ups of both men; the man in the dark blue shirt listens intently, occasionally nodding or gesturing, while Kashkari emphasizes his points with expressive hand gestures. Kashkari continues, asserting that Wall Street often believes prices are untethered from reality and that the Fed 'has their back,' leading to an unintended side effect of 'helping the rich,' despite well-intentioned policies. A monitor on a tripod in front of the man in the dark blue shirt displays a stylized, cartoonish version of their video call, offering a production perspective. The narrator concludes by stating that Kashkari's argument ignores the benefit to the poor, implying that ignoring this means only helping the wealthy.

event_blocks.summary: Neel Kashkari, President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures, discussed the 'incomplete analysis' of the Fed's choices, including interest rates and quantitative easing, emphasizing their impact not just on Wall Street or asset prices, but crucially on 'the men and women in America who are trying to find work' and 'deserve higher earnings,' stating he would 'take that tradeoff' if it benefits them. A 'whoosh' sound accompanied a transition to a black screen displaying 'CHAPTER NINE' and 'A Source of Instability,' with suspenseful, low background music beginning. The scene shifted to a nighttime city street where pedestrians walked past a large electronic stock ticker displaying real-time stock prices for companies like SONY, MA, and MCD, with green and red numbers indicating market movements. A male narrator's voice overlaid this, discussing the 'debate over the effects on Main Street' and 'increasing concerns about the risks on Wall Street,' pondering the fate of 'companies that had gone deep into debt' and their investors during a downturn. The camera then panned to a time-lapse of modern glass skyscrapers, transitioning from day to night with office lights illuminating the buildings, as the narrator continued, highlighting 'dire warnings' about a 'largely unregulated sector of the financial world' that became a 'key player.' Another male voice emphatically concluded, stating, 'Finance was getting bigger and bigger and riskier.'

He speaks with a clear, authoritative, and passionate voice about the Fed's tradeoffs, interest rates, quantitative easing, and the importance of helping American men and women find work and achieve higher earnings, stating he would 'take that tradeoff'.

event_blocks.summary: Mohamed A. El-Erian, Chief Economic Advisor at Allianz, explained to the camera that financial risk had migrated to unregulated 'non-banks,' altering the system and making it harder to comprehend, a concept he elaborated on as 'shadow banking' involving asset management companies and hedge funds that become systemically important despite lacking regulation. The FRONTLINE logo appeared, followed by aerial night shots of Manhattan's skyline and a zoom on the New York Stock Exchange. In a studio with exposed brick walls, Correspondent James Jacoby asked El-Erian about his concerns regarding shadow banks, to which El-Erian responded that the 'core of the problem... is that it's extremely fragile.' A narrator then introduced Lev Menand, a former economic advisor to the Fed and Treasury Department. Menand, also in a softly lit room with exposed brick, warned that 'shadow banks were largely untouched' by regulations and 'were endangering the whole system,' noting that investors have an 'incentive to withdraw' during 'little economic shocks,' potentially causing 'runs and panics.' Footage of the FRONTLINE documentary setup, including a monitor on a tripod, was shown before Menand continued, explaining how the shadow banking system has 'inserted a source of instability' that could 'throw us all off course.' The scene shifted to a July 2019 congressional hearing where Jerome Powell, identified as 'CHAIRMAN POWELL,' sat at a table, and a narrator stated the Fed was aware of 'potential instability posed by the shadow banking system.' Representative Jim Himes then spoke, followed by Powell asking, 'How are you thinking about potential risk bubbling up in the broader shadow banking system?'

The FRONTLINE logo appears briefly. Sweeping aerial night shots of Manhattan's illuminated skyline then showcase dense clusters of skyscrapers, leading to a zoom on the grand neoclassical New York Stock Exchange, illuminated in blue with a banner reading 'YOU BRING IT TO LIFE. WE BRING IT TO MARKET.' El-Erian reappears, continuing to describe 'shadow banking' as banking activities happening in the shadows, involving asset management companies and hedge funds that, despite being unregulated, become systemically important. The scene shifts to behind-the-scenes footage of a film crew, showing a camera operator monitoring a live feed on a monitor, capturing Correspondent James Jacoby in a modern studio with exposed brick walls. A wider shot reveals the professional lighting and equipment surrounding the interview space, as a narrator introduces 'Lev Menand, who'd been an economic advisor to the Fed and Treasury Department.'

Economist Lev Menand, formerly of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, is seen in a softly lit room with exposed brick walls, wearing glasses, a dark blazer, and a white collared shirt. He warns that despite regulations on big banks, "shadow banks were largely untouched" and "were endangering the whole system." He explains that investors in shadow banks have an "incentive to withdraw" during "little economic shocks" that cause asset prices to fall, potentially triggering "runs and panics." The camera briefly pulls back to reveal the FRONTLINE documentary setup, including a monitor on a tripod displaying Menand's live feed, a camera operator, and professional lighting equipment, before cutting back to Menand as he continues speaking, gesturing slightly, about how the shadow banking system has "inserted a source of instability" that could "throw us all off course." The scene then transitions to a formal congressional hearing in July 2019, where Jerome Powell, identified as 'CHAIRMAN POWELL', sits at a long wooden table in a dark suit and purple tie, reviewing documents amidst other officials. A narrator states that "potential instability posed by the shadow banking system was on the Fed's radar." Representative Jim Himes (D) of Connecticut is then shown speaking into a microphone, wearing a white shirt and purple tie, before the camera cuts back to Powell, who, with hand gestures, asks, "How are you thinking about potential risk bubbling up in the in the broader shadow banking system?" and begins to say, "this is this is a project that the..." in the formal wood-paneled hearing room.

event_blocks.summary: Jerome Powell, identified as 'CHAIRMAN POWELL', emphasized the critical need for global financial stability oversight, monitoring leverage and lending, during a formal hearing. Concurrently, a narrator highlighted the system's vulnerability to a shock by early 2020, underscored by a chapter title card reading 'Whatever It Takes'. The narrative then shifted to the emergence of a mysterious pneumonia outbreak in Wuhan, China, with medical personnel in protective suits attending to patients outside a hospital entrance, while news anchors reported on a "previously unknown coronavirus" originating from a "meat and seafood market," noting "45 cases... including two deaths" in China. Inside a chaotic Chinese hospital, masked patients and medical staff in white protective suits handled documents amidst signage like '发热门诊区' (Fever Clinic Area), reflecting "dire conditions." Neel Kashkari explained that the pandemic would drive the economy, a sentiment echoed by a female voice-over reporting a "major sell-off across Europe" as "investors are spooked by the growing number of infections outside China." Exterior shots of the New York Stock Exchange, the Charging Bull statue, and aerial views of Manhattan at dusk preceded Kashkari's observation of "maximum uncertainty" in financial markets due to the virus's speed and healthcare response. Richard Quest reported on 'CORONAVIRUS FEARS DRIVE STOCKS DOWN ALMOST 1,200 POINTS' on CNN, with a graph of the Dow Jones Industrial Average dropping significantly, as Quest detailed "concerns, fears, worries, and deep uncertainties." Inside the NYSE trading floor, traders in blue jackets and headsets worked intensely as Kashkari noted "people were scared," leading them to "just want cash," resulting in the "worst two-day point drop ever in history." The trading floor experienced further chaos with shouts of "They're on the bell!" as a CNBC graphic showed the S&P 500 plunging 7%, triggering a circuit breaker. Analytical voices described "all the weaknesses of the system that had built up over the years of easy money" being exposed and "market functioning was starting to cascade into failure." The Dow plunged again, ending an 11-year bull market, with credit markets seizing up and stocks in a "downward free fall." Trader 240 was seen typing rapidly amidst distress. Attention focused on "highly leveraged shadow banks," leading to a "full-blown panic in the shadow banking system." Kashkari concluded that the "bank panic" was caused by an "exogenous shock" combined with "underlying vulnerabilities in your monetary system," as traders continued to move quickly on the floor with screens displaying stock tickers like 'The Walt Disney Company'.
